questions.question: In which year did BakshiJagabandhulead thePaikaRebellion to protest against the British land revenue policy?
questions.solution:
Paika Rebellion (1817)

The Paika Rebellion was an armed uprising against the British East India Company's rule in Odisha in 1817. The Paikas were the traditional landed militia of Odisha. Under the leadership of Bakshi Jagabandhu, they revolted against the British due to the rise in salt prices, the abolition of the local currency (cowrie), and an oppressive land revenue policy that led to the loss of their ancestral lands.

questions.question: Why did Mahatma Gandhi travel to Noakhali in 1946?
questions.option_1: To lead a protest against British rule
questions.option_2: To promote communal harmony after religious violence
questions.option_3: To support the Bengal famine victims
questions.option_4: To organise the Quit India Movement
questions.solution:
Mahatma Gandhi's Visit to Noakhali

In 1946, during the period leading up to India's independence, the Noakhali district (now in Bangladesh) witnessed severe communal riots. Mahatma Gandhi traveled there to promote communal harmony and restore peace. He stayed in the region for four months, walking from village to village to offer solace to the victims and appeal for non-violence and religious unity.

questions.question: In which year was the Partition of Bengal, initially implemented by the British in 1905, officially annulled?
questions.solution:
Annulment of the Partition of Bengal

The Partition of Bengal, implemented by Lord Curzon in 1905, was officially annulled in 1911. The decision was announced by King George V during the Delhi Durbar. The annulment was a response to the massive protests and the Swadeshi Movement. Concurrently, the capital of British India was shifted from Calcutta to Delhi.

questions.question: In which year did Francois Martin establish Pondicherry, which later emerged as the Headquarters of the French in India?
questions.solution:
Foundation of Pondicherry

In 1674, Francois Martin, the first Governor General of French India, founded the settlement of Pondicherry (now Puducherry). Under his leadership, it transformed from a small fishing village into a major commercial port and eventually became the headquarters of the French East India Company in India.

questions.question: Who signed the Poona Pact of 1932 on behalf of Mahatma Gandhi, as he was imprisoned at the time?
questions.solution:
The Poona Pact (1932)

The Poona Pact was an agreement between Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and the upper-caste Hindu leaders regarding legislative seat reservations for the depressed classes. Since Mahatma Gandhi was on a fast-to-death in Yerwada Jail, Pt. Madan Mohan Malviya signed the pact on behalf of the caste Hindus/Gandhi on September 24, 1932.

questions.question: Which Princely State had a representative assembly from as early as 1881?
questions.solution:
Mysore Representative Assembly

The Princely State of Mysore was a pioneer in democratic reforms among the Indian states. It established a Representative Assembly as early as 1881. This allowed local representatives to voice their concerns and discuss the state's budget and administration, making it one of the most progressive princely states in British India.

questions.question: Which Princely State was ruled personally by the Nizam and had the 'Sarf Khas' estate?
questions.solution:
Hyderabad and the 'Sarf Khas'

The Nizam of Hyderabad was one of the wealthiest rulers in the world. The 'Sarf Khas' were the crown lands or the personal estates of the Nizam, which were managed separately from the state's general treasury. The revenue from these vast lands went directly to the Nizam's personal purse to maintain his household and palaces.

questions.question: Birjis Qadr, the son of the deposed Nawab Wajid Ali Shah, was proclaimed the new Nawab of a princely state in which place?
questions.solution:
Birjis Qadr and the Siege of Lucknow

During the 1857 Revolt, Birjis Qadr, the young son of the exiled Nawab Wajid Ali Shah and Begum Hazrat Mahal, was proclaimed the Nawab of Lucknow (Awadh) by the rebel forces. His mother, Begum Hazrat Mahal, took charge of the administration and led the resistance against the British in the region.

questions.question: Which of the following movements, though initially religious in nature, later evolved into a peasant movement under the leadership of Dudu Miyan, son of Haji Shariatullah?
questions.solution:
The Faraizi Movement

The Faraizi Movement was founded by Haji Shariatullah in East Bengal to purge Islam of un-Islamic practices. However, under his son Mohsinuddin Ahmad (Dudu Miyan), it evolved into a revolutionary peasant movement. Dudu Miyan organized the peasantry against the illegal cesses and exploitation by British indigo planters and local landlords.

questions.question: The Paramhansa Sabha was founded in:
questions.solution:
Paramhansa Sabha

The Paramhansa Sabha was a secret socio-religious society founded in 1849 in Bombay by Dadoba Pandurang and Durgaram Mehtaji. It was one of the earliest reform organizations in Maharashtra, focusing on the abolition of caste distinctions, the promotion of widow remarriage, and the worship of one God.

questions.question: Which of the following reformers was NOT associated with British India?
questions.solution:
Aryabhatta: Ancient Mathematician

Aryabhatta was a legendary Indian mathematician and astronomer from the Classical Age of Indian mathematics (around 476–550 CE). He was not associated with British India (which began in the 18th century). In contrast, Tilak, Raja Ram Mohan Roy, and Vivekananda were central figures of the modern Indian reform and nationalist movements.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of reform organisations and their founders is correctly matched?
questions.option_3: Arya Samaj – Raja Ram Mohan Roy
questions.solution:
Reform Organisations and Founders

The following table lists the correct founders for the mentioned organizations:

OrganisationCorrect Founder
Brahmo SamajRaja Ram Mohan Roy
Arya SamajDayanand Saraswati
Satya Shodhak SamajJyotiba Phule
Bahujan SamajMukundrao Patil / Bhaskararao Jadhav

questions.question: Who founded the Prarthana Samaj?
questions.solution:
Prarthana Samaj

The Prarthana Samaj (Prayer Society) was founded in 1867 in Bombay by Atmaram Pandurang. Inspired by the Brahmo Samaj, it sought to reform Hindu society through monotheism and social reforms like education for women and the abolition of the caste system. Its most famous leader was Mahadev Govind Ranade.

questions.question: Who started the Ramakrishna Mission?
questions.solution:
Ramakrishna Mission

The Ramakrishna Mission was founded by Swami Vivekananda in 1897. He named it after his guru, Ramakrishna Paramhansa. The mission's core philosophy is 'Atmano Mokshartham Jagat Hitaya Cha' (For one's own salvation and for the welfare of the world), combining spiritual practice with social service.

questions.question: Who is referred to as the 'Father of the Indian Renaissance' due to their contribution in social reform, literary works and unwavering patriotism?
questions.solution:
Father of the Indian Renaissance

Raja Ram Mohan Roy is known as the 'Father of the Indian Renaissance'. He was a pioneer of social reform who campaigned against Sati, child marriage, and polygamy. He founded the Brahmo Samaj and advocated for Western education and a scientific temper while maintaining Indian cultural roots.

questions.question: Which act stated that the Presidencies of Madras and Bombay were to be subordinate to the Presidency of Bengal in all matters of war, diplomatic relations and revenue?
questions.solution:
Pitt’s India Act, 1784

Pitt's India Act of 1784 established the supremacy of the British government over the East India Company's affairs. A significant provision was that the Presidencies of Madras and Bombay were made subordinate to the Governor-General of Bengal in matters related to war, peace, and revenue, creating a more centralized administration.

questions.question: According to the Regulating Act 1773, which of the following options is INCORRECT about the powers and responsibilities of the Governor General?
questions.option_1: Powered to superintendent the presidencies of Madras and Bombay in war and peace
questions.option_2: Can override the decision of his council
questions.option_3: Can control the presidencies of Madras and Bombay in war and peace
questions.option_4: Can make rules and regulations for the civil government of Fort William
questions.solution:
The Regulating Act of 1773

The Regulating Act of 1773 established the office of the Governor-General of Bengal. However, it did NOT allow the Governor-General to override his council. Decisions were made by a majority vote of the council. The power to override the council was only granted later, through the Amending Act of 1786 and the Charter Act of 1793.

questions.question: Which of the following acts reduced the number of directors of East India Company?
questions.solution:
Charter Act of 1853

The Charter Act of 1853 was the last of the series of Charter Acts. One of its key administrative changes was reducing the number of Directors of the East India Company from 24 to 18 (six of whom were to be nominated by the British Crown), signaling the diminishing power of the Company.

questions.question: Which event marked the final collapse of the British policy of annexation?
questions.solution:
End of the Policy of Annexation

The Revolt of 1857 was the turning point that ended the British policy of territorial expansion. Following the revolt, the Queen's Proclamation of 1858 explicitly stated that the British Crown would no longer annex Indian Princely States, abandoning policies like the 'Doctrine of Lapse'.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT considered one of the significant cause for the Revolt of 1857?
questions.option_2: Policies of British favouring sati, female infanticide and widow re-marriage
questions.option_3: Lack of contact between the ruler and the ruled
questions.solution:
Causes of the 1857 Revolt

The British policies favoring social reforms like the abolition of Sati, banning female infanticide, and supporting widow remarriage were resented by conservative sections of Indian society, but they were not the primary political or military triggers of the revolt. The core causes included the Doctrine of Lapse, economic exploitation, and the immediate cause: the greased cartridges.

questions.question: Which of the following key centres of the 1857 Revolt was recaptured from the rebel forces in September 1857?
questions.solution:
Recapture of Delhi (1857)

Delhi was the symbolic center of the 1857 Revolt. After a long and bloody siege, British forces under the command of John Nicholson (who died during the assault) successfully recaptured Delhi in September 1857. This victory was a major blow to the rebel cause and led to the arrest of the last Mughal Emperor, Bahadur Shah Zafar.

questions.question: The revolt of 1857 broke out on 10ᵗʰ May from which of the following places, when sepoys rose in rebellion and marched towards Delhi to restore Bahadur Shah II to the throne?
questions.solution:
The Outbreak at Meerut

The Revolt of 1857 officially began on May 10, 1857, in Meerut. The sepoys killed their European officers, released their imprisoned comrades, and marched through the night to Delhi. Upon reaching Delhi the next morning, they proclaimed the aging Mughal Emperor, Bahadur Shah II, as the Emperor of Hindustan.

questions.question: After the revolt of which year did the British implement measures that reduced the proportion of Indian soldiers in the army and increased the number of European soldiers?
questions.solution:
Post-1857 Military Reforms

After the 1857 Revolt, the British reorganized the Indian Army to prevent future uprisings. Based on the Peel Commission's recommendations, they reduced the ratio of Indian soldiers to European soldiers. In the Bengal Army, the ratio was fixed at 2:1, and in the Madras and Bombay armies, it was 3:1.

questions.question: Which of the following options is INCORRECT regarding the factors that inflamed the feelings of the Sepoys and led to the Revolt of 1857?
questions.option_1: Compulsion on sepoys to wear their caste marks and follow their own caste
questions.option_3: Army maintaining chaplains at state cost and promotion of Christian propaganda among the sepoys
questions.option_4: Act that required every new recruit to give an undertaking to serve overseas
questions.solution:
Factors Inflaming Sepoys

The 1857 Revolt was fueled by fears of religious conversion and loss of caste. The British actually prohibited sepoys from wearing caste marks or sectarian symbols while in uniform, which was seen as an interference in their religious identity. Statement 1 is incorrect because it suggests the British compelled them to follow their caste, while the opposite was true.

questions.question: The Poligars rebelled against the British in which Presidency, when the British tried to snatch away their military and land rights?
questions.solution:
The Poligar Rebellions

The Poligars (Palaiyakkarars) were feudal lords who held military and administrative powers in the Madras Presidency. They rebelled against the British East India Company between 1799 and 1805 (notably led by Veerapandiya Kattabomman) when the British attempted to disarm them and collect direct land revenue.
